1: The date header — “tenth year, tenth month, twelfth day” — corresponds to January 7, 587 BCE (Julian), approximately 18 months into Nebuchadnezzar's siege of Jerusalem (which began in December 589 BCE). This is the fifth of the six dated Egypt oracles and one of only three oracles in Ezekiel dated within the siege-period of 589-587 BCE (alongside 24:1 and Ezekiel 30:20). The context is critical: Jerusalem is surrounded, the Egyptian army under Hophra/Apries has moved toward Judah to relieve it (Jeremiah 37:5), and the oracle against Egypt arrives at precisely the moment when exiles might pin their hopes on Egyptian intervention.
2: “Set your face against Pharaoh” — the same confrontation formula used against Jerusalem (Ezekiel 6:2), Judah (Ezekiel 21:2), Ammon (Ezekiel 25:2), Tyre (Ezekiel 28:21), and Sidon (Ezekiel 28:22). By applying the identical formula to Egypt that YHWH used against his own people and their neighbors, Ezekiel places Egypt within the same theological framework: no nation stands outside YHWH's jurisdiction. The formula originated in Israelite siege rhetoric (the commander “set his face against” a fortified city) and became Ezekiel's characteristic expression of prophetic-divine opposition. “Prophesy against him and against all Egypt” extends the judgment from the individual ruler to the entire nation — Pharaoh's pride implicates Egypt.
3: Pharaoh king of Egypt is identified as “the great sea creature lying in the midst of his rivers” (hatannin hagadol harovets betokh yeoreiv). Tannin (sea creature/monster) is the chaos-dragon of ANE cosmology — cognate with the Ugaritic tnn who fights Baal, and related to the Babylonian Tiamat whom Marduk slays. In Egyptian iconography, Pharaoh was associated with the Nile crocodile (Sobek) and with the serpent-dragon Apophis, the enemy of Re. Ezekiel collapses these traditions: Pharaoh is a chaos creature whose domain is the Nile. The boast “My Nile is my own; I made it for myself” (li yeori vaani asitini) echoes the Pharaonic theology that the annual Nile flood was mediated by the king's divine status — the pharaoh's ritual power was believed to sustain the Nile's fertility. This is the precise claim YHWH will refute: the Nile belongs to YHWH, not Pharaoh, and YHWH will prove it by removing Pharaoh from it.
4: Fish-hook imagery (“I will put hooks in your jaws”) depicts YHWH as the divine fisher-king who hauls out the chaos monster from its native element. This inverts the normal ANE imagery where the great fish is a symbol of abundance and divine provision — here it becomes the means of humiliation. The fish clinging to Pharaoh's scales are his people and the armies dependent on him — they will be dragged out together. The same hook imagery appears in Amos 4:2 (Israel will be “taken away with hooks”) and in Ezekiel 38:4 (YHWH will put hooks in Gog's jaws). In Egyptian military iconography, prisoners were often depicted with hooks through their lips or noses — a deliberately humiliating reversal of Egyptian conquering-imagery.
5: Thrown into the wilderness — the reversal of the Exodus. In the Exodus, Israel was freed from Egypt and led through the wilderness to the land; here, Egypt's king is expelled from his river to the wilderness and left unburied. “Not be gathered or collected” (lo-teasef velo tikavets) — proper burial was a deep concern in the ancient world; unburied corpses were the ultimate disgrace (Deuteronomy 28:26; Jeremiah 7:33; Ezekiel 8:2). Leaving Pharaoh's body as “food to the beasts of the field and birds of the air” is the specific covenant-curse of Deuteronomy 28:26 applied to Egypt's king. The wilderness-exposure imagery also evokes the fate of Egypt's army in the Red Sea — another reversal: previously Egypt's enemies drowned in the sea; now Egypt is exposed in the desert.
6: “All the inhabitants of Egypt will know that I am the LORD, because they have been a staff of reed to the house of Israel” — the “knowledge formula” (veyadeu ki ani YHWH) appears 72 times in Ezekiel and marks every major judgment and restoration oracle. YHWH's actions produce knowledge of who he is — his identity is revealed through historical events. The “staff of reed” (mikkel qaneh) metaphor already appeared in the Assyrian threat to Hezekiah (2 Kings 18:21 = Isaiah 36:6): “you are relying on Egypt, that broken reed of a staff, which will pierce the hand of any man who leans on it.” Ezekiel applies the same image to Judah's continued reliance on Egypt despite that earlier warning — 130 years later, the same mistake, the same result.
7: The reed staff imagery is extended mechanically: when Israel “grasped you with their hand, you splintered and tore their shoulder.” Egypt's military intervention on Judah's behalf (Hophra's march toward Jerusalem in 588 BCE, documented in Jeremiah 37:5-11) briefly caused Nebuchadnezzar to lift the siege — but Egypt withdrew without engaging, and the siege resumed. The physical image (a walking-staff that splinters cuts the user's palm; a weight-bearing staff that breaks wrenches the shoulder) perfectly captures Egypt's half-hearted intervention: Egypt's support caused damage rather than help, because Hophra withdrew at the critical moment, forcing Judah to re-face the Babylonian siege alone and with diminished hope.
8: The “sword against you” formula (veheveiti aleykha cherev) is the divine-warrior declaration: YHWH himself is the attacker, using Nebuchadnezzar's sword as his instrument (made explicit in Ezekiel 29:19). The cutting off of “both man and beast” (adam uvehemah) mirrors the covenant-curse language of Leviticus 26:22 (beasts cutting off livestock) and Deuteronomy 28:31 (loss of animals). The destruction extends beyond human casualties to the agricultural economy — Egypt's cattle herds grazed on the Nile's floodplain, and their destruction signals the collapse of Egypt's economic foundations.
9: The knowledge formula (“they will know that I am the LORD”) appears again, this time grounding the judgment in Pharaoh's specific sin: the claim of Nile-ownership and self-creation. The theological point is sharp — Egypt's judgment is not random but proportionate: Egypt claimed divine powers over the Nile, and YHWH will demonstrate that the Nile is his to give or take. The same logic governs all the Egypt oracles: Egypt's fall is not merely political conquest but a demonstration of YHWH's exclusive sovereignty over creation, including the natural resources that Egypt falsely attributed to its own divine king.
10: “From Migdol to Syene, as far as the border of Cush” — Migdol (Migdol) was a fortress city on Egypt's northeastern border in the Sinai delta region (identified with Tell el-Borg or Tell el-Abqa); it was also the location where some Jewish refugees from Jerusalem settled after 586 BCE (Jeremiah 44:1). Syene (Aswan, at the first cataract of the Nile) was Egypt's southern border with Nubia/Cush, site of the famous Elephantine Jewish colony. The phrase is therefore equivalent to “from north to south” — the entire length of the Nile valley. The rhetorical gesture (naming the extreme boundaries) is the same as Israel's “from Dan to Beersheba” — totality expressed through polar extremes.
11: “No human foot will pass through it, no animal's foot will pass through it, and it will not be inhabited for forty years” — the forty-year desolation is the covenant-discipline number (forty years of wilderness wandering; forty days on Sinai; forty days of Elijah's journey). The desolation is total: no foot — animal or human — will traverse it. This hyperbolic language follows the pattern of the Sodom-and-Gomorrah oracle in Isaiah 13:20 (Babylon: “it will never be inhabited or lived in for all generations; no Arab will pitch his tent there”). Egypt was indeed weakened after Nebuchadnezzar's campaign (568-567 BCE) but was not literally uninhabited for forty years — the prophetic language uses covenant-number (forty) to signal the totality and divine purposiveness of the discipline.
12: The desolation of Egypt “in the midst of desolated lands” places Egypt within the broader landscape of Babylonian conquest — Judah, Phoenicia, and the surrounding nations were all in various states of devastation. Egypt's cities “among ruined cities” (bevein arim chorevot) reflects the actual condition of the eastern Mediterranean world after Nebuchadnezzar's campaigns: Tyre besieged for 13 years, Jerusalem destroyed, Ammon and Moab subjugated. The scattering of Egyptians among the nations mirrors exactly what happened to Israel — YHWH treats Egypt with the same covenant-curse logic he applied to his own people, universalizing the judgment structure.
13: “Yet” (ki koh amar Adonai YHWH veqibatzti) — the restoration promise interrupts the desolation oracle in the same pattern as the restoration promises within the Israel-judgment oracles (Ezekiel 11:17; Ezekiel 20:41-42; Ezekiel 36:24). Egypt will be gathered from the nations at the end of forty years — a reversal of its scattering. The “gathering from the peoples” (qibatzti) language is the same used for Israel's eschatological restoration (Ezekiel 20:34, 41; Ezekiel 28:25; Ezekiel 34:13; Ezekiel 36:24; Ezekiel 37:21; Ezekiel 38:8). YHWH's gathering formula is not exclusive to Israel — it belongs to his sovereign management of all nations, and Egypt receives a form of it, though on much reduced terms.
14: Restoration to “Pathros, the land of their origin” — Pathros (Hebrew Patros, Egyptian Pa-to-Res, “the southern land”) is Upper Egypt, the ancient heartland of Egyptian civilization centered on Thebes. The 26th Dynasty (the Saite period, ca. 664-525 BCE) had its origins in Lower Egypt (the Delta), ruling a reunified Egypt from Sais. Pathros as origin-land suggests Ezekiel envisions Egypt returning to its ancient core territory rather than dominating the Delta and the near eastern corridor. The “lowly kingdom” (mamlekhet shefalah) is not an insult but a categorical diminution: Egypt will persist as a nation but will never again be the regional hegemon that Israel's kings sought as an ally.
15: “The humblest of kingdoms, and it will no longer exalt itself over the nations” — this is fulfilled in broad historical outline. After the Babylonian and then Persian conquest (Cambyses invaded Egypt in 525 BCE), Egypt never again achieved independent regional hegemony. It remained a wealthy but politically subordinate province: Persian satrapy (525-332 BCE), Macedonian/Ptolemaic kingdom (332-30 BCE), and Roman province (30 BCE). The “I will diminish them” (umitatim) formula is the precise reversal of the reed-staff offense: Egypt claimed to prop up others; YHWH will make it so small it cannot prop up anyone, including itself.
16: “Egypt will no longer be a source of confidence for the house of Israel” (velo-yihyeh od leveit Yisrael limivtach) — mivtach (confidence/trust) is the wisdom vocabulary for misplaced reliance; Proverbs uses it for trusting in wealth (Proverbs 11:28), and the Psalms for trusting in anything other than YHWH (Psalms 118:8-9). Judah's chronic policy of seeking Egyptian alliance against Assyria and Babylon is the historical background: Isaiah 31:1 (“Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help”); Jeremiah 2:36 (“How cheap you made yourself by going to Assyria and Egypt”). The exile cure for this misplaced confidence is Egypt's reduction: when Egypt is a “lowly kingdom,” it will no longer tempt Israel to look southward instead of to YHWH.
17: The date shift — “twenty-seventh year, first month, first day” = April 26, 571 BCE — places this as the latest dated oracle in the entire book of Ezekiel, coming 16 years after the fall of Jerusalem and 26 years after Ezekiel's call. The insertion of this later oracle into the earlier Egypt collection (Ezekiel 29:1, dated 587 BCE) is unusual but intelligible: both concern Egypt's fate and Nebuchadnezzar's role. The occasion is the end of Nebuchadnezzar's 13-year siege of Tyre (ca. 585-573 BCE) — documented in Josephus who cites Phoenician records, and implied by Ezekiel's reference to bald heads and rubbed shoulders from the siege-labor.
18: “Every head went bald and every shoulder was rubbed raw” — a vivid description of the physical toll of siege warfare: soldiers wore helmets that abrared the scalp; carrying siege equipment (battering rams, lumber for siege towers, earth for ramps) rubbed shoulders raw. The Assyrian reliefs of siege operations (like the Lachish siege relief) show armies carrying exactly this kind of equipment. Nebuchadnezzar “received no payment from Tyre” because Tyre (built on an island offshore) was essentially impregnable in this period and eventually negotiated terms rather than surrendering its wealth intact. Alexander the Great finally took Tyre in 332 BCE by building a causeway — Nebuchadnezzar lacked the means to do so.
19: “I am giving the land of Egypt to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon” — the frank acknowledgment that Nebuchadnezzar served YHWH's purpose at Tyre but received no material reward, and that Egypt is promised as compensation, is remarkable for its theological honesty. It treats Nebuchadnezzar as a hired laborer (the imagery of payment, wages, and service is commercial/contractual) who has not yet received his due. The oracle is dated to 571 BCE, when Nebuchadnezzar was already engaged in his Egyptian campaign (568-567 BCE is the likely date of Nebuchadnezzar's actual invasion of Egypt, attested in fragmentary Babylonian records).
20: “I have given him the land of Egypt as his wage for what he did, because he and his army worked for me, declares the Lord GOD” — the theological claim here is audacious: Nebuchadnezzar's Babylonian army is described as YHWH's workforce (avad li, worked for me). This extends the Cyrus-as-shepherd logic of Isaiah 44:28 and Isaiah 45:1 backward: even before Cyrus, Nebuchadnezzar was YHWH's instrument. The king who destroyed Jerusalem, deported Judah, and besieged Tyre is described as working for YHWH — not despite his wickedness, but as the vehicle through which YHWH's judgment purposes were executed. This is consistent with Ezekiel's theology of YHWH's universal sovereignty over all nations' military history.
21: “On that day I will cause a horn to sprout for the house of Israel” — the horn (qeren) is the biblical symbol of royal power and strength (Psalms 92:10; Ezekiel 132:17; Ezekiel 148:14). A sprouting horn (atzmich qeren) is a Davidic/messianic image — Psalms 132:17 uses the same verb: “there I will make a horn sprout for David.” In the LXX and later tradition, this was read as messianic. The oracle thus ends with an unexpected messianic note: Egypt's humiliation and Nebuchadnezzar's wages are the context in which YHWH will restore Davidic strength to Israel. “I will open your mouth” refers to Ezekiel's prophetic speech being restored — 3:27 established his silence would end when YHWH opened his mouth again (fully enacted in Ezekiel 33:21-22 after Jerusalem's fall).
